claims.claim_text: The opening bank outage anecdote presents a real incident with specific timestamps, alert counts, and WAL replay duration.
claims.span_hint: line 26 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: At 03:14 on a Saturday, a regional bank's payment authorization service starts dropping transactions. The on-call SRE wakes to a phone full of pages: 312 alerts in the first nine minutes, fired by 312 separate routes because each microservice owns its own routing tree. Buried somewhere in that wall is the actual cause, but the engineer cannot find it because the central Prometheus has just OOM-killed itself trying to evaluate the dashboards everyone is opening, and the standby instance is in a 40-minute WAL replay loop with no query path. The metrics platform built to *show them* what was happening is the platform that is now *blind* during the incident, and the post-mortem will conclude that the observability stack added forty minutes to mean-time-to-resolution.
claims.suggested_rewrite: During a major incident, a poorly scaled observability stack can make triage slower by flooding responders with alerts and leaving dashboards unavailable at the worst moment.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with fake-precise operational details.

claims.claim_text: The module states that the opening scenario is a real, non-hypothetical incident.
claims.span_hint: line 28 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.suggested_rewrite: This failure pattern is common in large observability deployments.
claims.rationale: The text asserts a real incident but provides no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Teams hit single-node Prometheus limits past roughly 500 nodes or a few million active series.
claims.span_hint: line 28 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Every team that runs Prometheus past about 500 nodes, or past a few million active series, runs into a version of the same wall.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that run Prometheus at sufficiently large scale eventually hit limits in a single-node collection, storage, querying, and alerting architecture.
claims.rationale: The numeric thresholds are presented as fact without a primary source.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus local storage uses a TSDB with an in-memory head block, WAL, and regular block compaction.
claims.span_hint: lines 34-38 | section: The Limits of Single-Node Prometheus
claims.rationale: The shared Prometheus storage source directly covers local TSDB, WAL, head block, and block compaction behavior.

claims.claim_text: A series consumes roughly 3-4 KiB of RAM, so 15 million active series need about 50-60 GiB for the head.
claims.span_hint: line 36 | section: The Limits of Single-Node Prometheus
claims.anchor_text: A series consumes roughly three to four kilobytes of resident memory regardless of how often it is sampled. A cluster with fifteen million active series therefore needs fifty to sixty gigabytes of RAM just for the head, plus working memory for queries and rule evaluation, before you have left any headroom for the spikes that happen when a Deployment rolls and every pod gets a new `pod-template-hash` label.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Active-series count is one of the main drivers of Prometheus memory usage, so very large series counts can require tens of gigabytes of RAM before query and rule-evaluation overhead are added.
claims.rationale: The exact per-series RAM estimate and derived capacity numbers need a direct primary source.

claims.claim_text: Slow disks or network volumes cause the WAL to fall behind and produce `out of order sample` scrape errors.
claims.span_hint: line 38 | section: The Limits of Single-Node Prometheus
claims.anchor_text: On NVMe these operations are usually invisible, but on a spinning disk or a slow network volume the WAL falls behind the scrape, scrapes start dropping with `out of order sample` errors, and the data your alerts depend on is silently incomplete.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Fast local storage makes Prometheus compaction and WAL activity much less intrusive, while slow storage can increase ingest latency and make the monitoring data your alerts depend on less trustworthy.
claims.rationale: The precise failure chain to `out of order sample` errors is too specific for the cited storage overview.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus federation uses the `/federate` endpoint with `match[]` parameters and is intended for selected or aggregated time series in hierarchical setups.
claims.span_hint: lines 46-49 | section: Hierarchical Federation Versus Sharding
claims.rationale: The Prometheus federation documentation directly supports the endpoint and selected-series behavior, but prometheus.io is outside the prompt allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus `hashmod` relabeling sets a target label to the modulus of a hash of concatenated source labels.
claims.span_hint: lines 52-69 | section: Hierarchical Federation Versus Sharding
claims.rationale: The upstream Prometheus configuration reference documents the `hashmod` relabel action.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus Operator `shards:` uses target sharding and supports `__tmp_hash` for custom sharding behavior.
claims.span_hint: line 69 | section: Hierarchical Federation Versus Sharding
claims.rationale: The Prometheus Operator API reference documents sharding defaults and `__tmp_hash` customization.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus Agent mode was introduced in v2.32, enabled with `--enable-feature=agent`, and omits local blocks and local querying.
claims.span_hint: line 75 | section: Prometheus Agent Mode
claims.rationale: The Prometheus 2.32 changelog explicitly documents the Agent feature, flag, and remote-write-only behavior.

claims.claim_text: Thanos Sidecar uploads Prometheus TSDB blocks to object storage and exposes recent Prometheus data to Thanos Query via StoreAPI.
claims.span_hint: line 103 | section: Long-Term Storage: Thanos Versus Mimir
claims.rationale: The upstream Thanos Sidecar docs directly describe sidecar block upload and query access.

claims.claim_text: Grafana Mimir distributors validate writes, enforce tenant-specific limits, shard series across ingesters, and ingest data into Mimir.
claims.span_hint: line 105 | section: Long-Term Storage: Thanos Versus Mimir
claims.rationale: The Mimir distributor documentation directly supports the write-path, validation, tenant-limit, and sharding claims.

claims.claim_text: Grafana Mimir uses `X-Scope-OrgID` for tenant identity and native multi-tenancy.
claims.span_hint: line 105 | section: Long-Term Storage: Thanos Versus Mimir
claims.rationale: The Mimir authentication and authorization documentation directly discusses tenant IDs and the `X-Scope-OrgID` header.

claims.claim_text: Mimir shuffle sharding assigns tenants to deterministic subsets to reduce noisy-tenant blast radius.
claims.span_hint: lines 117 and 286 | sections: Long-Term Storage; Did You Know?
claims.rationale: Grafana's Mimir shuffle-sharding documentation directly describes tenant subset assignment and blast-radius reduction.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry Collector supports agent and gateway deployment patterns for node-local collection and centralized processing.
claims.span_hint: lines 123-127 | section: OpenTelemetry Collector Pipelines
claims.rationale: The shared OpenTelemetry deployment source directly covers agent and gateway Collector topologies.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry Collector pipelines are composed from receivers, processors, exporters, and service pipeline configuration.
claims.span_hint: line 127 | section: OpenTelemetry Collector Pipelines
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry Collector configuration documentation defines receivers, processors, exporters, and service pipelines.

claims.claim_text: The Collector batch processor should run after memory-limiter and sampling processors.
claims.span_hint: line 178 and line 299 | sections: OpenTelemetry Collector Pipelines; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The upstream batch processor README explicitly states that batch should be defined after `memory_limiter` and sampling processors.

claims.claim_text: The LGTM stack consists of Loki for logs, Grafana for visualization, Tempo for traces, and Mimir for metrics.
claims.span_hint: line 184 | section: The Grafana LGTM Stack
claims.rationale: Grafana's stack page identifies the LGTM components and their roles.

claims.claim_text: Loki indexes labels, not log-line content; high-cardinality labels increase stream count and degrade performance.
claims.span_hint: lines 188-190 | section: Loki: Logs Indexed By Label Only
claims.rationale: The Loki label documentation directly states that log content is not indexed and that high cardinality harms performance.

claims.claim_text: Loki keeps storage costs an order of magnitude lower than Elasticsearch on the same workload.
claims.span_hint: line 188 | section: Loki: Logs Indexed By Label Only
claims.anchor_text: This is how Loki keeps storage costs an order of magnitude lower than Elasticsearch on the same workload.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This is how Loki can keep storage costs substantially lower than full-text systems that index log content.
claims.rationale: The general cost principle is defensible, but the exact comparative multiplier needs a dedicated benchmark source.

claims.claim_text: Loki labels should have tens of distinct values, and labels with more than a few hundred distinct values will crash the ingester.
claims.span_hint: line 190 | section: Loki: Logs Indexed By Label Only
claims.anchor_text: The labels are for *infrastructure metadata* that has tens of distinct values across the entire cluster: namespace, app name, container, level. If you find yourself adding a label that would have more than a few hundred distinct values across the lifetime of a tenant, you are about to crash the ingester.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The labels are for bounded infrastructure metadata such as namespace, app name, container, and level. If you find yourself adding a label with unbounded or rapidly growing values over a tenant's lifetime, you are likely to create a high-cardinality stream problem.
claims.rationale: The numeric threshold and guaranteed crash outcome are too precise for the available Loki cardinality documentation.

claims.claim_text: Tempo uses Parquet-based block storage and TraceQL for querying traces.
claims.span_hint: line 194 | section: Tempo: Traces At Object-Storage Cost
claims.rationale: The Tempo Parquet documentation directly states that Parquet blocks enable search and TraceQL.

claims.claim_text: Tempo can only be looked up directly by trace ID.
claims.span_hint: line 194 | section: Tempo: Traces At Object-Storage Cost
claims.anchor_text: Like Loki, Tempo trades expensive indexes for cheap storage: the only thing you can look up directly is a trace ID, and richer queries scan over Parquet using TraceQL's filter pushdown.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Like Loki, Tempo trades heavyweight indexing for cheaper storage: exact trace IDs are easy to retrieve, and richer searches rely on TraceQL over Parquet-backed trace data.
claims.rationale: Current Tempo supports broader TraceQL-based search, so the trace-ID-only wording is too absolute.

claims.claim_text: Grafana can display metric exemplars as diamonds, and exemplars can carry trace IDs for correlation from metrics to traces.
claims.span_hint: line 196 | section: Tempo: Traces At Object-Storage Cost
claims.rationale: Grafana's signal-correlation documentation shows exemplars carrying trace IDs and appearing as diamond points.

claims.claim_text: Pyroscope supports continuous profiling, language SDKs, and eBPF-based profiling options.
claims.span_hint: line 200 | section: Pyroscope: Continuous Profiling
claims.rationale: Grafana Pyroscope language SDK documentation lists SDK and eBPF profiling options.

claims.claim_text: Prometheus supports scrape-side limits such as `sample_limit`, `label_limit`, `label_name_length_limit`, and `label_value_length_limit`.
claims.span_hint: lines 217-224 | section: Cardinality Management
claims.rationale: The Prometheus configuration reference directly documents these scrape limit fields and their failure behavior.

claims.claim_text: Mimir per-tenant limits reject excess ingestion with HTTP 429 responses.
claims.span_hint: line 228 and line 295 | sections: Cardinality Management; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The Mimir distributor documentation covers tenant-specific limit enforcement and ingestion rejection behavior.

claims.claim_text: Alertmanager supports routing, grouping, and inhibition of alerts.
claims.span_hint: lines 236-240 | section: Alerting Fatigue Mitigation
claims.rationale: The upstream Alertmanager README and example configuration directly describe routing, grouping, and inhibition.

claims.claim_text: The Prometheus TSDB two-hour head block was chosen to balance commodity memory with common query windows, and changing it is inadvisable.
claims.span_hint: line 285 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: The Prometheus TSDB's two-hour head block is not arbitrary. It was chosen because two hours is short enough to keep the in-memory index manageable on commodity hardware, and long enough that a typical query window (last hour, last thirty minutes) almost always lives entirely in the head, avoiding any disk read at all. Changing it is technically possible but operationally inadvisable.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Prometheus organizes ingested samples into two-hour TSDB blocks, with the current head kept in memory until it is compacted.
claims.rationale: The current two-hour block fact is supportable, but the historical design explanation and operational judgment need a source stronger than the storage overview.

claims.claim_text: Shuffle sharding was originally formalized by AWS for SQS and is widely used in multi-tenant data systems.
claims.span_hint: line 286 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: The technique was originally formalized by AWS for SQS and is now used widely in multi-tenant data systems.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The technique is a common way to reduce noisy-neighbor blast radius in multi-tenant systems.
claims.rationale: AWS documents shuffle sharding for fault isolation, but the specific SQS-origin claim is not supported by the located primary source.

claims.claim_text: Tempo's storage format started as bespoke and migrated to Parquet so the same files could be queried by Apache DataFusion.
claims.span_hint: line 287 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Tempo's storage format started as bespoke and migrated to Parquet so that the same files could be queried by general-purpose tools like Apache DataFusion. This is part of a wider pattern in observability: backends are increasingly settling on Parquet plus object storage as a common substrate, because it is cheap and lets independent tools share data without re-ingestion.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Tempo now uses Parquet-based blocks in object storage, reflecting a broader observability trend toward cheap object storage plus columnar formats for large-scale telemetry data.
claims.rationale: The current Parquet fact is supportable, but the migration history and DataFusion rationale are not directly backed by the cited docs.

claims.claim_text: The `prometheus_remote_storage_dropped_samples_total` counter is under-watched, and any non-zero rate should page.
claims.span_hint: line 288 | section: Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: The `prometheus_remote_storage_dropped_samples_total` counter is one of the most under-watched metrics in the ecosystem. When it is non-zero, your remote-write pipeline is silently losing data; alerts that depend on the dropped data may never fire, and dashboards can show an apparent return to baseline that is in fact a measurement gap. Treat any non-zero rate on this counter as a paging-grade event.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A remote-write sample-drop counter is one of the key metrics to watch. When it increases, your remote-write pipeline is losing samples; alerts that depend on the dropped data may never fire, and dashboards can show an apparent return to baseline that is in fact a measurement gap. Treat a sustained non-zero rate as urgent until you understand the cause.
claims.rationale: The exact metric name and the 'under-watched' superlative are not adequately supported for the Prometheus version context.

claims.claim_text: Running multiple Thanos Compactors against the same bucket is unsafe because the compactor is singleton-by-design.
claims.span_hint: line 300 | section: Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The upstream Thanos Compactor documentation states that compact is not concurrency safe and must run as a singleton against a bucket.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table repeats the unsupported exact remote-write drop counter name for Prometheus Agent WAL outages.
claims.span_hint: line 296 | section: Common Mistakes
claims.anchor_text: | Running Prometheus Agent mode without sizing the WAL for expected network outages | When remote-write is unreachable, the WAL fills, and once full the agent silently drops new samples; you do not detect this until the dashboard returns from the dead with a hole in it | Size the WAL volume for the longest tolerable network partition; alert on `prometheus_remote_storage_dropped_samples_total` and on WAL disk usage |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Running Prometheus Agent mode without sizing the WAL for expected network outages | When remote-write is unreachable, the WAL can exhaust its available buffer and new samples may be dropped; you may not notice until dashboards show a gap after recovery | Size the WAL volume for the longest tolerable network partition; alert on remote-write sample-drop metrics and WAL disk usage |
claims.rationale: The row combines a strong data-loss claim with an exact metric name that is not safely supported for the module's Prometheus context.

further_reading.title: Prometheus Storage
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary reference for TSDB head behavior, WAL replay, block compaction, and single-node storage constraints.

further_reading.title: Deploy the OpenTelemetry Collector
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers agent, gateway, and split-tier Collector deployment patterns used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: Grafana Mimir Architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains Mimir's distributor, ingester, querier, store-gateway, compactor, and multi-tenant architecture.

further_reading.title: Loki Cardinality
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly explains why high-cardinality labels create stream explosions and operational pain in Loki.

further_reading.title: Prometheus Alertmanager
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for Alertmanager routing, grouping, inhibition, and notification behavior.

notes: No claim entries were emitted for quiz prompts/answers, Mermaid diagrams, code blocks, or routine exercise commands except where prose/table rows made factual vendor, statistic, or incident-style claims requiring citation or removal.
